era_context: Late 12th century. The Crusader states are declining; Saladin is consolidating Ayyubid power. In Europe, feudalism is at its height with powerful regional lords. In Asia, the Jin Dynasty controls northern China; the Khmer Empire is flourishing; Japan is in the Genpei War. Islamic civilization is experiencing a cultural and scientific flourishing.

events.title: Genpei War: Minamoto Victories in Eastern Japan
events.description: In 1182, the Genpei War (1180–1185) was in full swing, with Minamoto no Yoritomo's forces gaining ground against the Taira clan in eastern Japan. The year 1182 saw several Minamoto victories that consolidated their control of the Kanto region and surrounding territories. These military successes were crucial to Yoritomo's eventual triumph and the establishment of the Kamakura shogunate. The Genpei War represented a fundamental shift in Japanese politics, from the dominance of the imperial court and Fujiwara regency to the rise of military government (bakufu). The conflict also saw the development of samurai military culture and the codification of warrior ethics.

sources.name: Genpei Seisui Ki (Record of the Genpei War)

events.certainty_note: The Genpei War is well-documented, but the specific chronology of individual battles and campaigns within 1182 is less precise. The overall trajectory is secure, but exact dates may vary by ±1–2 years.
cross_references: 1185-001 (End of Genpei War and establishment of Kamakura shogunate)

events.title: Saladin's Consolidation of Ayyubid Power
events.description: In 1182, Saladin (Ṣalāḥ ad-Dīn Yūsuf ibn Ayyūb) was consolidating his control over the Ayyubid confederation, unifying Egypt, Syria, and parts of Mesopotamia under his authority. This consolidation was essential to his later campaigns against the Crusader states. By 1182, Saladin had eliminated rival Ayyubid princes and established a centralized military and administrative structure. His unification of the Islamic world in the Levant and Egypt created the military and economic resources necessary for the major offensives against the Crusaders that would culminate in 1187. Saladin's consolidation also represented a shift from fragmented regional powers to a unified Islamic state capable of coordinated military action.

sources.name: Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani, Conquest of Jerusalem
Baha ad-Din Ibn Shaddad, Life of Saladin

events.certainty_note: Saladin's consolidation of Ayyubid power is well-documented in contemporary Islamic sources. The process spanned several years, with 1182 marking a significant phase in this consolidation.

events.title: Frederick Barbarossa's Continued Rule of Holy Roman Empire
events.description: In 1182, Frederick I Barbarossa was in the 28th year of his reign as Holy Roman Emperor (1152–1190). The year 1182 falls within a period of relative stability in the empire, though Frederick was increasingly focused on Italian affairs and the conflict with the Papacy. His reign was characterized by attempts to strengthen imperial authority, though the power of the German princes was growing. Frederick's later years would see his involvement in the Third Crusade, which would ultimately lead to his death in 1190. In 1182, the empire was at a high point of territorial extent and administrative organization, though the seeds of future fragmentation were already present.
events.certainty_note: Frederick Barbarossa's reign and the general political situation in 1182 are well-documented in contemporary German chronicles.

events.title: Jayavarman VII's Reign in Khmer Empire
events.description: In 1182, Jayavarman VII had recently ascended to the throne of the Khmer Empire (c. 1181) and was beginning his reign of architectural and religious transformation. By 1182, he was initiating the major construction projects that would define his reign, including the expansion of Angkor Thom and the construction of the Bayon temple. Jayavarman VII was a Mahayana Buddhist who transformed the empire's religious landscape and expanded its territorial control. His reign represented the apex of Khmer civilization in terms of architectural achievement, administrative sophistication, and territorial extent. The empire controlled much of mainland Southeast Asia and maintained extensive trade networks with China and other regions.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Angkor Thom and Bayon

events.certainty_note: Jayavarman VII's ascension is traditionally dated to c. 1181, making 1182 early in his reign. The chronology is secure within ±1–2 years based on inscriptional evidence.

events.title: Jin Dynasty Stability in Northern China
events.description: In 1182, the Jin Dynasty (established by the Jurchen in 1115) maintained firm control over northern China under Emperor Shizong (reigned 1161–1189). The Jin Dynasty was at a relatively stable point, with a well-organized administrative system and military structure. The division of China between the Jin Dynasty in the north and the Song Dynasty in the south was a defining feature of 12th-century East Asian geopolitics. The Jin Dynasty represented a significant non-Han conquest state and facilitated cultural and technological exchange between the steppe and settled Chinese civilization. The Jin court at Zhongdu (modern Beijing) was a center of cultural and intellectual activity.
key_figures: Jin court officials and military commanders

sources.name: Song Dynasty records and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: The political situation in 1182 is well-documented in both Jin and Song records. The Jin Dynasty's control of northern China is securely established.

disconfirming_evidence: No significant disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed for 1182. The chronology of the Genpei War and Saladin's consolidation of power are well-established in modern scholarship, though the specific dating of individual battles and campaigns within 1182 may vary slightly by ±1–2 years.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1182 is dominated by European, Islamic, and East Asian sources, reflecting the documentary traditions of literate societies. Japanese sources provide detailed accounts of the Genpei War, though the narrative tradition (Heike Monogatari) was compiled centuries later and may contain legendary elements. Islamic sources on Saladin are contemporary and relatively reliable. Chinese and Korean records are well-preserved. The sources reflect elite concerns—military campaigns, dynastic politics, and religious projects—and likely underrepresent economic and social developments among non-elite populations. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania have no written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Minamoto victories in 1182 culminated in the establishment of the Kamakura shogunate in 1185
Saladin's consolidation of Ayyubid power in 1182 enabled his conquest of Jerusalem in 1187
